MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its input-output combination against a reasonable set of unsupervised baselines (vector offset/arithmetic, difference, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) on the same SemEval 2010 Task 8 data using consistent evaluation (modified purity) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair comparisons. The authors acknowledge when prior methods are competitive and analyze how element-wise similarity and second-order signals contribute to results. However, from the summary it appears there are minor omissions: comparisons to other embedding variants or supervised relation classifiers and release of code/models are not mentioned, so the experimental comparison is good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against the principal and recent unsupervised compositional baselines (vector offset/arithmetic, difference, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) on the same data (ukWaC embeddings and SemEval 2010) with consistent metrics, which makes the comparisons largely fair. It explicitly acknowledges when offsets and multiplicative methods perform comparably or have specific weaknesses and analyzes how its input-output combination differs from and improves on those approaches. Given the summary notes that related work coverage is adequate but no code release is mentioned and supervised or more recent relation-representation alternatives are not discussed, the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive, hence a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its input-output combination against a reasonable set of unsupervised baselines (vector offset/arithmetic, difference, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) on the same SemEval 2010 Task 8 data using consistent evaluation (modified purity) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair comparisons. The authors acknowledge when prior methods are competitive and analyze how element-wise similarity and second-order signals contribute to results. However, from the summary it appears there are minor omissions: comparisons to other embedding variants or supervised relation classifiers and release of code/models are not mentioned, so the experimental comparison is good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against the principal and recent unsupervised compositional baselines (vector offset/arithmetic, difference, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) on the same data (ukWaC embeddings and SemEval 2010) with consistent metrics, which makes the comparisons largely fair. It explicitly acknowledges when offsets and multiplicative methods perform comparably or have specific weaknesses and analyzes how its input-output combination differs from and improves on those approaches. Given the summary notes that related work coverage is adequate but no code release is mentioned and supervised or more recent relation-representation alternatives are not discussed, the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive, hence a 4.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it tackles semantic relation representation with large-scale word2vec models and unsupervised clustering evaluated on SemEval 2010 Task 8, using expected baselines (offsets, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) and reasonable hyperparameter reporting. The audience (embedding/representation and relation-learning researchers) is appropriate and the experimental setup (ukWaC training, CBOW vectors, clustering with Cluto) matches community standards, though the paper would be stronger for a top-tier acceptance if code/models and exact clustering scripts/splits were released to ensure full reproducibility and if the modified purity metric were more thoroughly justified or accompanied by standard complementary metrics. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP research problem—representing semantic relations with word embeddings—and evaluates on a standard benchmark (SemEval 2010 Task 8) using large-scale unsupervised experiments, which fits well within top NLP/ML venue scope. The methodology (comparing multiple composition methods, using ukWaC-trained CBOW vectors, and introducing second-order similarity) and the clustering-based evaluation are appropriate for the intended audience of semantic-relation and representation researchers. Minor scope concerns arise from the purely unsupervised focus and the absence of mentioned code release, which may limit immediate reproducibility and applied impact, but these do not outweigh its relevance to the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it tackles semantic relation representation with large-scale word2vec models and unsupervised clustering evaluated on SemEval 2010 Task 8, using expected baselines (offsets, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) and reasonable hyperparameter reporting. The audience (embedding/representation and relation-learning researchers) is appropriate and the experimental setup (ukWaC training, CBOW vectors, clustering with Cluto) matches community standards, though the paper would be stronger for a top-tier acceptance if code/models and exact clustering scripts/splits were released to ensure full reproducibility and if the modified purity metric were more thoroughly justified or accompanied by standard complementary metrics.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP research problem—representing semantic relations with word embeddings—and evaluates on a standard benchmark (SemEval 2010 Task 8) using large-scale unsupervised experiments, which fits well within top NLP/ML venue scope. The methodology (comparing multiple composition methods, using ukWaC-trained CBOW vectors, and introducing second-order similarity) and the clustering-based evaluation are appropriate for the intended audience of semantic-relation and representation researchers. Minor scope concerns arise from the purely unsupervised focus and the absence of mentioned code release, which may limit immediate reproducibility and applied impact, but these do not outweigh its relevance to the venue.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—unsupervised identification of semantic relations in embedding spaces—using large-scale experiments on ukWaC and SemEval 2010, which is valuable for researchers working with distributional representations. Its empirical finding that element-level similarities explain much of analogy success and that an input–output combination improves clustering performance offers useful, actionable insights and a new representation that outperforms common baselines in unsupervised clustering. However, the contribution is primarily empirical and narrowly scoped (unsupervised clustering on one benchmark, no mention of released code), so while it will inform future embedding and relation-representation work, it is unlikely to fundamentally change how the field models relations without further validation and broader applicability studies. | B(3): The paper tackles an important problem—representing semantic relations with word embeddings—and provides useful empirical insights (e.g., that much of vector-arithmetic analogy success stems from individual-element similarity and that second-order/input–output combinations can improve unsupervised clustering on SemEval 2010 data). These findings help reframe how researchers should interpret offsets and suggest a practical representation (input–output combination) that outperforms common baselines for relation clustering. While not transformative, the work offers a moderate-impact contribution that clarifies prior assumptions and opens concrete follow-on work on second-order relational representations and their applicability to downstream relation tasks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—unsupervised identification of semantic relations in embedding spaces—using large-scale experiments on ukWaC and SemEval 2010, which is valuable for researchers working with distributional representations. Its empirical finding that element-level similarities explain much of analogy success and that an input–output combination improves clustering performance offers useful, actionable insights and a new representation that outperforms common baselines in unsupervised clustering. However, the contribution is primarily empirical and narrowly scoped (unsupervised clustering on one benchmark, no mention of released code), so while it will inform future embedding and relation-representation work, it is unlikely to fundamentally change how the field models relations without further validation and broader applicability studies.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—representing semantic relations with word embeddings—and provides useful empirical insights (e.g., that much of vector-arithmetic analogy success stems from individual-element similarity and that second-order/input–output combinations can improve unsupervised clustering on SemEval 2010 data). These findings help reframe how researchers should interpret offsets and suggest a practical representation (input–output combination) that outperforms common baselines for relation clustering. While not transformative, the work offers a moderate-impact contribution that clarifies prior assumptions and opens concrete follow-on work on second-order relational representations and their applicability to downstream relation tasks.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): From the summary, the paper conducts a large-scale unsupervised empirical study and proposes an input-output combination of word2vec vectors that outperforms other simple combination heuristics on SemEval 2010 relation clustering. This constitutes a non-trivial empirical contribution and a useful perspective (emphasizing second-order similarity and the role of individual element similarities) but does not introduce a fundamentally new problem or a radically new modeling paradigm. The work reads as an incremental but informative advance—carefully differentiating itself from prior offset/multiplicative methods—rather than a highly novel theoretical or methodological breakthrough. | B(3): The paper offers a moderate novelty: it brings a large-scale, systematic empirical investigation showing that much of analogy success comes from individual-element similarities and proposes using second-order similarity plus an input–output (IO) combination of word2vec vectors for relation representations. These ideas build on well-studied vector-offset and input/output embedding literature (e.g., word2vec CBOW) but extend them non-trivially by applying second-order similarity to entity-pair representations and demonstrating improved unsupervised clustering on SemEval 2010. The contribution is clearly differentiated from prior work and provides a useful, though incremental, advance rather than a wholly new paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: From the summary, the paper conducts a large-scale unsupervised empirical study and proposes an input-output combination of word2vec vectors that outperforms other simple combination heuristics on SemEval 2010 relation clustering. This constitutes a non-trivial empirical contribution and a useful perspective (emphasizing second-order similarity and the role of individual element similarities) but does not introduce a fundamentally new problem or a radically new modeling paradigm. The work reads as an incremental but informative advance—carefully differentiating itself from prior offset/multiplicative methods—rather than a highly novel theoretical or methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a moderate novelty: it brings a large-scale, systematic empirical investigation showing that much of analogy success comes from individual-element similarities and proposes using second-order similarity plus an input–output (IO) combination of word2vec vectors for relation representations. These ideas build on well-studied vector-offset and input/output embedding literature (e.g., word2vec CBOW) but extend them non-trivially by applying second-order similarity to entity-pair representations and demonstrating improved unsupervised clustering on SemEval 2010. The contribution is clearly differentiated from prior work and provides a useful, though incremental, advance rather than a wholly new paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with large-scale unsupervised experiments on ukWaC and SemEval 2010, comparing multiple combination methods (input-output, offsets, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) and reporting hyperparameters and evaluation metrics. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines, but the summary does not mention statistical significance tests, release of code or exact splits for reproducibility, and some causal claims (e.g., why offsets fail at scale) would benefit from ablation or sensitivity analyses. The authors acknowledge dataset-specific limitations, and the results appear to justify the central conclusions though minor gaps in reproducibility and statistical analysis prevent a flawless score. | B(4): The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with a large-scale unsupervised experiment using CBOW vectors from ukWaC and clustering on SemEval 2010 Task 8, comparing several sensible baselines (offsets, difference, multiplicative, addition, concatenation). There are no novel mathematical proofs to vet and the reported experimental setup (vector sizes, hyperparameters, baselines) is appropriate, but the evaluation relies on a modified purity metric without mention of statistical significance testing or code release, which leaves some reproducibility and robustness questions. Overall the results appear to justify the main conclusions that offset alone is insufficient and that input–output and second‑order similarity combinations improve relational clustering, but minor methodological gaps prevent a flawless technical verdict.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with large-scale unsupervised experiments on ukWaC and SemEval 2010, comparing multiple combination methods (input-output, offsets, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) and reporting hyperparameters and evaluation metrics. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines, but the summary does not mention statistical significance tests, release of code or exact splits for reproducibility, and some causal claims (e.g., why offsets fail at scale) would benefit from ablation or sensitivity analyses. The authors acknowledge dataset-specific limitations, and the results appear to justify the central conclusions though minor gaps in reproducibility and statistical analysis prevent a flawless score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with a large-scale unsupervised experiment using CBOW vectors from ukWaC and clustering on SemEval 2010 Task 8, comparing several sensible baselines (offsets, difference, multiplicative, addition, concatenation). There are no novel mathematical proofs to vet and the reported experimental setup (vector sizes, hyperparameters, baselines) is appropriate, but the evaluation relies on a modified purity metric without mention of statistical significance testing or code release, which leaves some reproducibility and robustness questions. Overall the results appear to justify the main conclusions that offset alone is insufficient and that input–output and second‑order similarity combinations improve relational clustering, but minor methodological gaps prevent a flawless technical verdict.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs a large-scale unsupervised experiment on ukWaC and SemEval 2010, compares a wide set of vector-pair compositions (addition, concatenation, difference, multiplicative, input–output) and reports hyperparameters, which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. However, I see limited evidence of targeted ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., seed variability, statistical significance, or controlled removal of components) and code release is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility. Related work appears adequately covered and the authors discuss several important failure modes and limitations of offsets and multiplicative methods. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but falls short of the thorough robustness and reproducibility checks I expect for a top-tier experimental claim. | B(4): The paper runs a large-scale unsupervised experiment on ukWaC and SemEval 2010 with multiple composition methods (offsets, input-output, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) and evaluates via a modified purity metric, which provides solid empirical support for its main claims. It reports hyperparameters and compares reasonable baselines, and explicitly discusses limitations and challenging relation types. However, the work appears to lack thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of vector size, clustering settings, or corpus variations) and does not mention released code, which weakens exact reproducibility and the assessment of robustness. Overall the experiments are strong but miss some important analyses that would make the conclusions more fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs a large-scale unsupervised experiment on ukWaC and SemEval 2010, compares a wide set of vector-pair compositions (addition, concatenation, difference, multiplicative, input–output) and reports hyperparameters, which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. However, I see limited evidence of targeted ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., seed variability, statistical significance, or controlled removal of components) and code release is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility. Related work appears adequately covered and the authors discuss several important failure modes and limitations of offsets and multiplicative methods. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but falls short of the thorough robustness and reproducibility checks I expect for a top-tier experimental claim.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs a large-scale unsupervised experiment on ukWaC and SemEval 2010 with multiple composition methods (offsets, input-output, multiplicative, addition, concatenation) and evaluates via a modified purity metric, which provides solid empirical support for its main claims. It reports hyperparameters and compares reasonable baselines, and explicitly discusses limitations and challenging relation types. However, the work appears to lack thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of vector size, clustering settings, or corpus variations) and does not mention released code, which weakens exact reproducibility and the assessment of robustness. Overall the experiments are strong but miss some important analyses that would make the conclusions more fully convincing.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (unsupervised large-scale study, second-order similarity, and the input-output combination). Notation and hyperparameters are reported and appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective and informative. The writing quality is good with adequate related-work coverage, though some minor presentation polish could make technical details (e.g., limitations and clustering evaluation) even clearer to reproduce. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and easy to follow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (unsupervised large-scale experiment, role of second-order similarity, and a new input-output composition). Notation and methodological details (model, vector sizes, corpora, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and figures/tables appear informative for the SemEval clustering experiments. Minor presentation issues remain: the modified purity metric and the exposition of second-order similarity could be explained more clearly for readers outside the immediate subfield, and it would help to explicitly state code availability. Overall, writing quality is good with only small clarity gaps.

Notation and methodological details (model, vector sizes, corpora, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and figures/tables appear informative for the SemEval clustering experiments. Minor presentation issues remain: the modified purity metric and the exposition of second-order similarity could be explained more clearly for readers outside the immediate subfield, and it would help to explicitly state code availability. main_claims: Presented an unsupervised large-scale experiment to identify relational similarities in word embedding compositions.
Confirmed that much of the success attributed to vector arithmetic analogies arises from similarities between individual elements.
Showed that incorporating second-order similarity can improve relational similarity and provide a more meaningful representation for entity pairs in semantic relations.
Proposed an input-output combination of word2vec vectors that outperforms other vector combination methods for unsupervised clustering on SemEval 2010 Relation Classification data.
_summary.methodology: Train a word2vec CBOW model on the ukWaC corpus, extract input and output vectors (size 400), construct various vector-pair combination representations (addition, concatenation, difference, multiplicative, and input-output combination), and perform unsupervised clustering (Cluto) on SemEval 2010 Task 8 relation instances, evaluated with a modified purity metric.
limitations: Unsupervised relation classification is challenging due to lexical vs contextual relation instances and their differing distributional cues.
Some relations have semantically constrained arguments while others have freer arguments, complicating clustering.
Vector offset method is inefficient at capturing generic semantic relations at large scale; offsets are not constant enough for unsupervised clustering.
Multiplicative methods capture some relational information but lose precision from individual similarities.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic relation classification / word embeddings

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: ML engineer and NLP experimentalist with extensive experience in embedding training, clustering algorithms, and reproducible evaluation of large-scale experiments.
traits: carefully checks whether experimental baselines, hyperparameters, and preprocessing steps are fully specified
requires ablation studies and controlled comparisons to support causal claims about method components
looks for statistical significance, robustness checks across datasets/seed variability, and sensitivity analyses
values reproducibility: expects code, trained models, and exact data splits or scripts to be released
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental design, implementation details, and whether the results are robust, reproducible, and fairly compared to appropriate baselines.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Relations Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in lexical semantics and distributional representations, with a track record of work on semantic relation classification, analogy tasks, and SemEval-style evaluations.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior work on vector arithmetic, second-order similarity, and relation representations
assesses whether the proposed ideas advance conceptual understanding of semantic relations and their representation
examines the significance and generality of the findings for downstream tasks and future research directions
notices whether the evaluation metric and experimental setup reflect real-world or linguistic desiderata (e.g., relation granularity, interpretability)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes positioning within the literature, conceptual contribution, and the broader impact and applicability of the proposed representations for semantic relation research.
